forecasts.reasoning: Only 24 days remain until the July 1, 2025 deadline. While John Morgan is exploring a run and must meet a June 12 statutory deadline to register his proposed new centrist party (to fulfill the 365-day affiliation requirement for a June 2026 qualifying date), he has explicitly distanced himself from an early formal declaration. In mid-May 2025, Morgan stated he is waiting to see how the gubernatorial field shapes up and emphasized that he can wait longer than other candidates to formally open a campaign account due to his existing immense statewide name recognition. With other main candidates like independent Jason Pizzo not expected to announce until September 2025, it is highly likely Morgan will use the June 12 deadline to launch the party while deferring his actual gubernatorial announcement until late 2025 or early 2026. Therefore, an announcement of his candidacy before July 1 is highly unlikely.

With the forecast date at June 6, 2025 and resolution on July 1, 2025, there are only 24 days remaining for either party to announce the cancellation of the agreement. The summary of recent evidence indicates that despite Bulgargaz stopping payments back in July 2024 and arrears reaching BGN 300 million by May 2025, neither side has officially cancelled the contract. Bulgargaz appears hesitant to unilaterally terminate due to the staggering estimated penalty of BGN 3 to 4.2 billion, opting instead to demand renegotiation (which BOTAŞ rejected) and prepare for likely arbitration. BOTAŞ, conversely, appears content to let the unpaid invoices accumulate rather than terminate the 13-year contract. Given the protracted nature of this dispute and the immense financial risks involved for the cancelling party, an official announcement of cancellation within the brief 24-day window before July 1, 2025 is highly improbable. I estimate a 6% chance as a baseline risk for unpredictable political or legal escalation over the next three weeks.

The ongoing devastating civil war in Sudan (since April 2023) has severely impaired government functionality. As of February 2025, Sudan had not ratified the AfCFTA and was still lacking requisite tariff offers. With the knowledge cutoff at June 6, 2025, there are only 25 days left until the July 1, 2025 deadline. It is highly unlikely that a fractured government embroiled in a severe conflict will complete the complex technical and political steps required to ratify an international free trade agreement in this very short remaining window, or has recently done so without notice.
